stories.title: Jason Alexander: Seinfeld actor apologises to Courtney Stodden for 'inappropriate' sketch when she was 17
stories.summary: Seinfeld actor Jason Alexander has apologised to reality star Courtney Stodden for an "inappropriate" comedy sketch they appeared in together when she was 17. The 2012 Funny or Die sketch, which parodied daytime talk shows, saw Alexander rubbing a mobile phone on Stodden's breasts while making suggestive remarks. Alexander has offered his "sincerest apologies" and expressed regret for his actions in the sketch.

stories.title: Tennis: Valentin Vacherot Falls in the First Round at Kitzbühel Against Quentin Halys
stories.summary: Valentin Vacherot, seeded No. 2, was eliminated in the first round of the ATP 250 in Kitzbühel, Austria, by Quentin Halys. The Frenchman won the match, with Vacherot struggling due to a stress fracture in his foot and bone edema. This setback comes after Vacherot's recent comeback in Gstaad, where he won a round before losing to Raphaël Collignon.

stories.title: UK millionaires ask Andy Burnham to tax them more
stories.summary: A group of 120 UK millionaires, including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno, have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed more, specifically a 2% tax on wealth over £10m, to promote a more equal society and devolve wealth and power from the richest. This move is part of a renewed call for higher taxes on the wealthy, following similar campaigns in previous years. The prime minister has not ruled out a wealth tax, suggesting he may consider implementing it.

stories.title: River levels approach all-time low as drought announcement looms
stories.summary: River levels in Wales are approaching all-time lows, with the country experiencing just 5% of its average July rainfall due to consecutive heatwaves and a dry spring. This has led to significant problems, including fish and wildlife deaths, failing crops, and heat-stressed livestock. An announcement from Natural Resources Wales is expected later on Thursday, as the region moves closer to an environmental drought.

stories.title: The music in Nolan’s Odyssey is as well-crafted – and bloodlessly unmemorable – as the movie itself
stories.summary: Christopher Nolan’s The Odyssey features Ludwig Göransson’s score, including a theme for the ancient aulos flute, inspired by recent research reconstructing 2,000-year-old Greek music. Critics note the score is polished yet unmemorable, aligning with mixed reviews of the film’s direction. The soundtrack’s subtle approach contrasts with Nolan’s usual immersive scores.

stories.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
stories.summary: The U.S. and Saudi Arabia signed a landmark nuclear cooperation deal on Wednesday, allowing Riyadh to develop a civilian nuclear program with expanded access for American companies. The agreement includes a bilateral safeguards pact to prevent proliferation, though details remain undisclosed. Saudi Arabia called it an extension of existing energy ties, as analysts warn the deal could set a precedent for future uranium enrichment in the region.

stories.title: Framework’s premium laptop is shipping with less RAM
stories.summary: Framework has raised prices on its upcoming Laptop 13 Pro after a memory supplier doubled costs, pushing the base prebuilt model to $1,599. Some preorder customers will receive adjusted memory configurations and must accept the change or cancel for a full refund. New orders now reflect the updated pricing and available specs.

stories.title: No men's Ashes Test in north of England - full 2027 schedule
stories.summary: England’s men’s Ashes defence starts at Trent Bridge on 18 June 2027, with no Tests in the north of England after Old Trafford and Headingley were left out. The five-Test series runs until 29 July, overlapping with the women’s multi-format Ashes from 24 June to 20 July. England will warm up against Bangladesh at Lord’s from 28 May, while Australia faces England Lions at Old Trafford from 7 June.

stories.title: Around 12,000 people evacuated in France fires
stories.summary: Around 12,000 people have been evacuated from the south-west of France due to ongoing wildfires, with over 2,000 hectares of land destroyed in the Gironde region. No injuries have been reported, but the use of internal combustion and electric engines has been banned in predominantly forested areas. The French government is working to contain the fires, which come after two firefighters died near Bordeaux airport.

stories.title: No 2027 men's Ashes Test in north of England
stories.summary: England’s men’s Ashes series against Australia in 2025 will open at Trent Bridge on 18 June, with no Tests in the north of England after Old Trafford and Headingley were dropped. The five-match series runs until 29 July, overlapping with the women’s Ashes from 24 June to 20 July. England will warm up with a Test against Bangladesh at Lord’s starting 28 May.

stories.title: 'Even the CEO doesn't know' - Kaymer on LIV's future
stories.summary: LIV Golf's team championship is highly unlikely to take place this year, with players including captain Martin Kaymer uncertain about the circuit's future beyond 2026. The tour is facing financial difficulties after Saudi Arabian backing was withdrawn, with CEO Scott O'Neil seeking $350 million in investment to keep it running. LIV Golf's next events will proceed as scheduled, starting with a tournament in Staffordshire on Thursday.

stories.title: Venice Film Festival Reveals 2026 Lineup (Updating Live)
stories.summary: The 2026 Venice Film Festival will open with the world premiere of Danny Boyle's "Ink," a 1960s-set biographical drama starring Guy Pearce as Rupert Murdoch and Jack O'Connell as Larry Lamb. The film, which also competes for the Golden Lion, marks a lighter Hollywood presence at the festival compared to previous years. The festival will close with Giovanni Veronesi's "Dio Ride," an out-of-competition film starring Pierfrancesco Favino as a 17th-century friar.

stories.title: Government to cut business rates for pubs, clubs and music venues
stories.summary: The government will slash business rates for pubs, clubs and music venues in England by 20% from April, saving firms around £1,100 each. The £100m package, announced by Andy Burnham, aims to protect 32,000 venues but excludes the largest live music spots. Details on eligibility will come at the autumn Budget.

stories.title: Daniel Wu on ‘Cold War 1994,’ ‘Twilight of the Warriors’ and Coming Home to Hong Kong: ‘The Craft Still Challenges Me’
stories.summary: Hong Kong actor Daniel Wu has received the Best from the East Award at the New York Asian Film Festival, capping a successful stretch in the "Cold War" series, including the latest installment "Cold War 1994," a prequel to the 2012 and 2016 films. The new film, set in the run-up to Hong Kong's 1997 handover, marks Wu's return to the franchise after a brief hiatus due to the pandemic. Wu will next be seen in "Twilight of the Warriors," another major Hong Kong production.

stories.title: U.S.-Saudi Deal Prompts Fears of a Nuclear Arms Race
stories.summary: In Europe, Asia and the Middle East, countries seek nuclear capabilities to hedge against aggressive neighbors and a receding Washington, potentially creating a more freewheeling era.

stories.title: A New Target for Ukraine’s Long-Range Attacks: Russia’s Version of Amazon
stories.summary: Kyiv is escalating its air war by striking commercial warehouses, trying to bring the conflict closer to home for ordinary Russians.

stories.title: A Peptide Showdown: F.D.A. Scientists May Clash With R.F.K.’s Agenda
stories.summary: Agency scientists are advising against allowing widespread use of several peptide injections. Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the health secretary, has said people should be free to experiment.

stories.title: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs
stories.summary: Google's parent company Alphabet has reported its first negative free cash flow in at least a decade, coming in at $5.9 billion, due to growing spending on artificial intelligence infrastructure. This spending is expected to reach $205 billion this year, a significant increase from $190 billion. As a result, Alphabet's stock fell 4% in after-hours trading.

stories.title: Nuclear Energy Revival Puts Westinghouse in Prime Position
stories.summary: The company, which filed for bankruptcy protection in 2017, stands to benefit from growing support for nuclear power and President Trump’s deal with Saudi Arabia.

stories.title: Should Travelers Give Up on Summer in Western Europe Because of the Heat?
stories.summary: A favorite vacation option may be at risk because of rising temperatures. Even those sticking with their summer plans agree: More air conditioning would help.

stories.title: As Cyclosporiasis Spreads, America Says No Thanks to Salads
stories.summary: Foot traffic to chains such as Chopt and Chipotle fell in recent weeks after a recall from Taylor Farms and changing messages from the F.D.A.

stories.title: How to Use ChatGPT and Gemini Prompts to Find Out What They Know About You
stories.summary: It can be unsettling what Gemini and ChatGPT have figured out about you and how easily your privacy can be punctured. Here’s how to find out.

stories.title: Russia's businesses under strain from Ukraine's attacks on Wildberries
stories.summary: Ukrainian drones have attacked several warehouses belonging to Russia's biggest online retailer, Wildberries, in the past few days, resulting in the deaths of eight workers and injuring dozens more. The attacks have disrupted supplies to the Russian army and also affected hundreds of Russian sellers who depend on online marketplaces for their livelihoods. The consequences of these attacks are expected to be felt by small and medium-sized businesses, with estimated losses of £750m.

stories.title: 'I am still alive': Indian activist on hunger strike for 26 days loses 11kg
stories.summary: Indian activist Lobsang Wangchuk has been on a hunger strike for 26 days, losing 11 kilograms, as protests against exam paper leaks continue in India. The demonstrations have sparked public anger and posed a challenge to Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government, with Modi attempting to reach out to students through a recent statement on social media. The protesters are demanding answers on the causes of the paper leaks and those who benefit from them.

stories.title: Butterfly city blooms in shadow of office blocks
stories.summary: In Hull city centre, a "butterfly city" has been created with the discovery of ten species of butterfly, including the rare small skipper, in a "mini meadow" established in 2023. The meadow, located near St Stephen's Shopping Centre, features grass and wild flowers that provide a habitat for the butterflies. This initiative encourages people to grow plants that support city centre wildlife.

stories.title: Alarm over hospital lung admissions for children in England’s poorest postcodes
stories.summary: Children in England’s poorest areas are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for respiratory infections than those in wealthier areas. In 2024/25, 1,941 per 100,000 children in the most deprived fifth of England were hospitalised compared to 1,380 per 100,000 in the least deprived. Experts warn air pollution and deprivation may be key factors as officials review long-term health strategies.

stories.title: Olympic Mountain Glory
stories.summary: A striking new image from the International Space Station highlights the rugged Olympic Mountains in northwest Washington state. The composite photo, taken July 23, 2026, captures snow-capped peaks, deep river valleys, and glaciers within Olympic National Park. The ancient basalt rock forming these peaks emerged from undersea eruptions between 55 and 15 million years ago.

stories.title: Life-changing MS drug to help people walk now on NHS in England
stories.summary: The first drug to help people with multiple sclerosis walk is now available on the NHS in England. Up to 5,000 people could benefit each year from fampridine, which improves walking speed and endurance. The NHS will now cover the cost of the drug, previously paid for privately by some patients.
